intro: Based in Porto and currently studying at FEUP, I work between art and technology, always looking for ways to bring the two together and create something new.
Driven by a strong commitment to design, I see each project as an opportunity to refine both my technical skills and aesthetic voice, with the goal of making every new piece better than the last.
skills: Design: Figma, Adobe Creative Suite (Ps, Ai, Id), Procreate.
Motion & 3D: Blender, Autodesk Maya, After Effects, Premiere Pro, Final Cut Pro, DaVinci Resolve.
Development: C# (Unity), Java, JavaScript, C/C++ (Arduino), HTML5/CSS3, GDScript.

quem-me-viu-e-quem-me-ve.title: quem me viu e quem me vê
quem-me-viu-e-quem-me-ve.caption: Poster cover developed for Interactive Narratives.

body.text: quem me viu e quem me vê was developed for the Interactive Narratives course, in the context of a short writing assignment for which the cover could also be approached creatively. I used that opening to design a poster that could extend the tone of the text rather than simply present it.

Produced in Adobe Photoshop, the piece focuses on typography as atmosphere. The blurred script in the background was used not only as a visual texture, but as a way of suggesting memory, distance, and emotional residue, while the more direct foreground title anchors the composition.

The work reflects an interest in treating editorial design as part of storytelling itself. Instead of separating text and image into fixed roles, the project explores how type, framing, and visual softness can participate in narrative construction.

Although developed within an academic brief, it became a project I was especially proud of, precisely because it felt like a small but meaningful exercise in authorship, restraint, and visual interpretation.

images.alt: Poster cover for quem me viu e quem me ve

away-with-the-fairies.title: Away with the Fairies
away-with-the-fairies.caption: Poster and the gate reference that inspired it.

body.text: Away with the Fairies is a poster developed in Adobe Illustrator from an observational starting point: a gate on the FEUP campus whose ornamental structure suggested a strong graphic language. Using the Rectangle Grid Tool as a generative base, the project translates that architectural reference into a more stylised visual composition.

The work explores how a real element from the built environment can be abstracted into a creative graphic system, balancing repetition, symmetry, and decorative rhythm with a more contemporary poster sensibility. Rather than reproducing the gate literally, the intention was to reinterpret it and build an image that feels both structured and atmospheric.

As an exercise, the project reflects an interest in pushing visual references beyond documentation and towards authorship, treating design as a space for interpretation, refinement, and aesthetic ambition.
It is a small but formative study in observation, abstraction, and composition, and in using digital tools not only for execution, but for discovering visual possibilities within an initial idea.

images.alt: Gate render that inspired the poster

body.text: FM is my personal brand in development, and this project brings together the different iterations of my website as part of that ongoing process. More than a single interface, it functions as a record of how I have been refining the way I present myself, my work, and my visual language.

Each version began in Figma, where I could test hierarchy, typography, layout, and rhythm, before being translated into code. That movement between mock-up and implementation was essential, since the project is as much about design decisions as it is about learning how to build them with precision.

At its core, FM reflects a strong investment in self-improvement and self-curation. I see personal branding not as surface styling, but as a continuous process of clarifying intention, sharpening taste, and making sure the work is presented with the same level of care that goes into creating it.

The selected screens show that evolution through different approaches to navigation, editorial structure, and tone, while maintaining the same underlying objective: to create a portfolio that feels authored, coherent, and increasingly aligned with the standard I want my work to meet.

images.alt: FM editorial sidebar dark mode concept screenshot

jenkins.title: Jenkins / 5 Shades of Pink
captions: Generative video built in Processing from audio-reactive inputs.

bodyFirst.text: Jenkins was developed for the course unit Programming III, whose proposal was to create a program in Processing that produces a sequence of generative images to create a real-time video based on the user's sound input.
In other words, the goal was to create a video with a mixed methodology that explores the aesthetic and technical possibilities of Processing.

It was developed entirely in Processing, an open-source programming language and integrated development environment. The code incorporates various inputs: the letters S, D, and H; the arrow keys; the numbers from 1 to 5; the left mouse button; and mouse dragging. By pressing keys 1 to 5, different images from the film Marie Antoinette (2006) appear, overlap, and disappear when the same keys are pressed again.

bodyMiddle.text: The output includes graphic animations that react to sound, such as a sound wave, triangles generated through mouse movement, circles that appear when the D key is pressed, random geometric shapes generated by clicking the H key, circles that appear with the S key and are removed by the mouse, and circles triggered by the arrow keys.

This project was done in collaboration with my colleague Alice Logvina. We worked together to produce the various components in Processing. She handled the 3D objects like the cube and the terrain, and I handled the programming of the other elements.

bodySecond.text: Later, in collaboration with the Interface Design course unit, this project evolved into an interactive installation using Processing and an Arduino simultaneously, thus creating 5 Shades of Pink.
Me and my colleague Alice Logvina once again worked together, and we both worked on all components such as coding in Processing and utilizing the Arduino.

For this project, we used the graphic developments from our midterm work and introduced interactivity through Arduino and a webcam. The webcam, detecting light, generates small triangles in the illuminated area. The cube also rotates depending on the position of the light. In addition, with the Arduino, we had 4 buttons in the center of the room to switch between these components.

body.text: Afterglow was developed in the final semester of the Multimedia degree as an individual project that combines game design, illustration, interface design, animation, and programming. The project explores how a point-and-click structure can support a calm and immersive experience centered on atmosphere rather than urgency.

Set in a visually stylized world inspired by cozy gaming and contemporary visual culture, the game invites players to move through spaces at their own pace, discovering interactions through observation and curiosity. Instead of directing the player through explicit instructions, the experience encourages intuitive exploration.

This approach is reinforced by a mostly diegetic interface, where information emerges from the game world itself. By reducing external UI noise and relying on visual cues, the project prioritizes emotional continuity, detail, and a sense of presence.

Mechanically, Afterglow remains intentionally light: players navigate environments, inspect objects, and uncover hidden elements, with progression built around free-form interaction rather than task pressure. The absence of strict goals or tutorials turns replayability into a natural consequence of discovery.

Conceptually, the project also reflects on gender representation, accessibility, and the value of aesthetics in game design. It positions visual sensibility as a legitimate design language and contributes to a broader conversation about more diverse, welcoming forms of play.

As a final outcome, Afterglow became both a personal and academic milestone: a cohesive project that demonstrates technical growth while preserving a clear artistic identity across illustration, design, sound, and interaction.

bodyFirst.text: Serpentina was developed for the course unit Digital Games, where we were challenged to add a meaningful twist to a classic arcade title. We chose Snake, a game with a familiar top-down 2D structure and a clear rhythm of movement, growth, and survival, and reimagined that rhythm through a single disruptive mechanic.

bodySecond.text: Players control the snake with W/A/S/D or arrow keys and collect strawberries that appear randomly inside the arena. Each strawberry increases the snake by one segment, but every three strawberries the controls invert, so up becomes down and left becomes right. That shift forces players to constantly remap their thinking, while still preserving the recognizable Snake loop of avoiding walls and self-collision.

Using the MDA perspective, our goal was to move the aesthetic experience away from steady mastery and toward adaptation, tension, and cognitive disruption. Rather than relying only on increasing snake length for difficulty, we introduced periodic uncertainty and turned movement itself into a strategic decision about timing, positioning, and risk. Even with a relatively small code intervention, the experiential impact was significant, aligning with our intent to make minimal mechanical changes with maximum effect.

Technically, we implemented this in Godot's 2D framework with a strawberry counter that triggers inversion at every multiple of three, a dynamic input remapping layer that switches between normal and reversed controls, and state persistence until the next threshold.

Our intended outcomes were to amplify cognitive challenge, introduce productive frustration, and increase replayability by ensuring that inversion moments happen in different spatial contexts each run. We also expected the mechanic to have a cognitive-training quality, since players must interrupt learned motor patterns and continuously rebuild control logic in real time.

Playtesting largely confirmed those goals. Players quickly recognized the mental challenge dimension, and although early runs produced frequent failures during inversion phases, that difficulty increased engagement rather than causing abandonment. Overall, Serpentina achieved the desired transformation: it kept the identity of Snake intact while creating a more adaptive, unpredictable, and cognitively demanding experience.
